subject: Trusts and Estates
coverage_standard: NCBE-aligned Trusts and Estates rule bank. Effective July 2026, Trusts and Estates is removed from the MEE subject list, but NCBE says it will still be tested regularly through the MPT from July 2026 through February 2028 and may continue to appear in skills-based contexts with provided materials.

rules.title: Testamentary Capacity
rules.prompt_question: What capacity is required to make a valid will?
rules.rule_statement: A testator has testamentary capacity if, at the time the will is executed, the testator is of the required age and understands the nature of making a will, the general nature and extent of the property, the natural objects of bounty, and the disposition being made.
rules.application_example: An elderly person with some memory loss may still have capacity if, when signing, the person understands family members, assets, and the will's effect.
rules.common_trap: Capacity is measured at the time of execution, not before or after.

rules.title: Insane Delusion
rules.prompt_question: When does an insane delusion invalidate a will?
rules.rule_statement: A will or provision may be invalid if it was the product of an insane delusion, meaning a false belief to which the testator adheres against all reason and evidence, and the delusion materially affected the testamentary disposition.
rules.application_example: A testator disinherits a child based on an irrational fixed belief that the child is a spy despite overwhelming evidence otherwise.
rules.common_trap: A mistaken belief is not enough; the belief must be irrational and causally connected to the gift.

rules.title: Undue Influence
rules.prompt_question: What is undue influence in the law of wills?
rules.rule_statement: A will or provision is invalid for undue influence if the influencer exerted such pressure that it overcame the testator's free will and caused a disposition the testator otherwise would not have made.
rules.application_example: A caretaker isolates a frail testator, threatens abandonment, and procures a large gift under a new will.
rules.common_trap: Motive, opportunity, or a close relationship alone are not enough without proof the influence actually overpowered the testator's mind.

rules.title: Presumption of Undue Influence
rules.prompt_question: When does a presumption of undue influence arise?
rules.rule_statement: A presumption of undue influence may arise when a beneficiary had a confidential relationship with the testator, actively participated in procuring the will, and unduly benefited from it.
rules.application_example: A lawyer-beneficiary drafts the will for a dependent client and receives the bulk of the estate.
rules.common_trap: The exact elements vary by jurisdiction, but confidential relationship plus suspicious circumstances is the classic pattern.

rules.title: Fraud in the Execution
rules.prompt_question: What is fraud in the execution of a will?
rules.rule_statement: Fraud in the execution occurs when the testator is deceived about the nature of the document being signed, such that the testator does not realize it is a will or does not understand its contents.
rules.application_example: A person is tricked into signing a will while being told it is a power of attorney.
rules.common_trap: This differs from fraud in the inducement, where the testator knows it is a will but is misled into making a gift.

rules.title: Fraud in the Inducement
rules.prompt_question: What is fraud in the inducement of a will?
rules.rule_statement: Fraud in the inducement occurs when the testator knowingly executes a will but is induced by a knowingly false representation to make a disposition the testator would not otherwise have made.
rules.application_example: A beneficiary lies that the testator's child stole money, causing the testator to cut the child out of the will.
rules.common_trap: The key distinction from undue influence is deceit rather than pressure.

rules.title: Duress
rules.prompt_question: When is a will invalid for duress?
rules.rule_statement: A will or provision is invalid for duress if the testator was forced to make the disposition by a wrongful threat that overcame the testator's free will.
rules.application_example: A person threatens physical harm unless the testator signs a will leaving everything to the threatening party.
rules.common_trap: Mere persuasion or emotional appeal is not duress.

rules.title: Formal Requirements for an Attested Will
rules.prompt_question: What are the usual formal requirements for an attested will?
rules.rule_statement: An attested will generally must be in writing, signed by the testator or by another at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence, and signed by the required number of witnesses within the time and manner required by statute.
rules.application_example: A testator signs a typed will before two witnesses who then sign as required.
rules.common_trap: Witness presence requirements differ by statute, so use the general rule unless the question specifies otherwise.

rules.title: Signature by Another on Testator's Behalf
rules.prompt_question: When may someone else sign a will for the testator?
rules.rule_statement: A will may be signed by another person on the testator's behalf if done at the testator's direction and in the testator's conscious presence or as otherwise allowed by statute.
rules.application_example: A physically disabled testator directs a friend to sign the will while the testator watches.
rules.common_trap: The substitute signer must act under the testator's authority and in the required presence.

rules.title: Conscious Presence Test
rules.prompt_question: What is the conscious presence test for will execution?
rules.rule_statement: Under the conscious presence test, a person signs in the testator's presence if the testator, through sight, hearing, or general awareness, comprehends that the signing is occurring nearby.
rules.application_example: A witness signs at the same table while the testator can hear and understand what is happening.
rules.common_trap: Some jurisdictions use narrower line-of-sight rules, but modern formulations often use conscious presence.

rules.title: Interested Witness
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of an interested witness?
rules.rule_statement: At common law, a gift to an interested witness could be void unless there were sufficient disinterested witnesses, but many modern statutes purge only the excess gift or abolish the rule entirely.
rules.application_example: A witness who is also a beneficiary may lose part or all of the gift depending on the statute.
rules.common_trap: Do not assume the entire will is invalid because of an interested witness.

rules.title: Holographic Will
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a valid holographic will?
rules.rule_statement: A holographic will is generally valid if the material portions are in the testator's handwriting and the document is signed by the testator, if recognized by the jurisdiction.
rules.application_example: A testator handwrites and signs a note leaving property to a friend.
rules.common_trap: Not all jurisdictions recognize holographic wills, and some require testamentary intent to appear on the face of the document.

rules.title: Harmless Error Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the harmless error rule for wills?
rules.rule_statement: Under the harmless error doctrine, a document not executed in strict compliance with will formalities may still be treated as a will if there is clear and convincing evidence that the decedent intended the document to constitute the decedent's will or amendment.
rules.application_example: A will signed by only one witness may be admitted if strong evidence shows the decedent intended it as a final will.
rules.common_trap: This doctrine is not universal, so use it only where recognized.

rules.title: Integration of a Will
rules.prompt_question: What is integration of a will?
rules.rule_statement: Integration allows all papers present at execution that the testator intended to be part of the will to be treated together as one will.
rules.application_example: Several stapled pages signed and witnessed together may be read as one integrated will.
rules.common_trap: The documents generally must be present at execution and intended to form one testamentary instrument.

rules.title: Republication by Codicil
rules.prompt_question: What is republication by codicil?
rules.rule_statement: A valid codicil republishes the will as of the date of the codicil, so the will is treated as reexecuted on that later date for many purposes.
rules.application_example: A codicil executed after the birth of a child may affect the child's treatment under pretermitted-heir rules.
rules.common_trap: Republication by codicil can change how after-acquired property or later events affect the will.

rules.title: Incorporation by Reference
rules.prompt_question: When may a document be incorporated into a will by reference?
rules.rule_statement: A separate writing may be incorporated by reference into a will if it existed at the time the will was executed, the will manifests intent to incorporate it, and the writing is sufficiently described to permit identification.
rules.application_example: A will refers to 'the memorandum dated January 1 in my desk drawer' and incorporates it if the requirements are met.
rules.common_trap: The separate document usually must already exist when the will is executed unless a statutory exception applies.

rules.title: Acts of Independent Significance
rules.prompt_question: What are acts of independent significance?
rules.rule_statement: A will may dispose of property by reference to acts or events that have significance apart from affecting the testamentary disposition, such as a gift to 'the employees at my death' or to 'the car I own at death.'
rules.application_example: A testator leaves a car to a niece, and the niece receives whichever car the testator happens to own at death.
rules.common_trap: The external act must have a lifetime purpose independent of changing the will's gifts.

rules.title: Pour-Over Gift to Trust
rules.prompt_question: When is a pour-over devise to a trust valid?
rules.rule_statement: Under the Uniform Testamentary Additions to Trusts Act and similar statutes, a will may validly pour property over to an inter vivos trust identified in the will, even if the trust is amendable or revocable and even if unfunded during life.
rules.application_example: A will leaves the residue to the settlor's revocable living trust.
rules.common_trap: At common law, pour-over gifts created incorporation issues, but modern statutes broadly validate them.

rules.title: Revocation by Physical Act
rules.prompt_question: How may a will be revoked by physical act?
rules.rule_statement: A will may be revoked by a subsequent physical act, such as burning, tearing, canceling, obliterating, or destroying, performed with the intent to revoke by the testator or at the testator's direction and in the testator's presence.
rules.application_example: A testator tears a signed will in half intending to revoke it.
rules.common_trap: The physical act alone is not enough without simultaneous intent to revoke.

rules.title: Revocation by Subsequent Instrument
rules.prompt_question: How may a will be revoked by a later writing?
rules.rule_statement: A will may be revoked by a later will or codicil executed with testamentary formalities if the later instrument expressly revokes the prior will or revokes it by inconsistency.
rules.application_example: A new will states, 'I revoke all prior wills and codicils.'
rules.common_trap: A later instrument may revoke only inconsistent provisions rather than the entire will unless the intent to revoke fully is clear.

rules.title: Partial Revocation by Physical Act
rules.prompt_question: May a will be partially revoked by physical act?
rules.rule_statement: In some jurisdictions, a will may be partially revoked by a physical act performed with intent to revoke that part, but other jurisdictions allow only total revocation by act.
rules.application_example: A testator scratches out one bequest in a will, and the effect depends on the jurisdiction's rule.
rules.common_trap: Always note that partial revocation by act is jurisdiction dependent.

rules.title: Dependent Relative Revocation
rules.prompt_question: What is dependent relative revocation?
rules.rule_statement: Dependent relative revocation allows a court to disregard a revocation if the testator revoked a will or gift based on a mistaken assumption of law or fact and would not have revoked but for that mistake, provided disregarding the revocation better carries out the testator's probable intent.
rules.application_example: A testator destroys an old will believing a new will is valid, but the new will fails for lack of witnesses.
rules.common_trap: DRR is an equitable doctrine used only when the court can infer the testator preferred the earlier disposition to intestacy or another result.

rules.title: Revival of a Revoked Will
rules.prompt_question: When is a revoked will revived?
rules.rule_statement: If a later will that revoked an earlier will is itself revoked, the earlier will is revived only if the testator intended revival or if revival is permitted under the applicable statute and supported by the circumstances.
rules.application_example: A testator revokes Will 2 after realizing it is defective and may or may not revive Will 1 depending on intent and statute.
rules.common_trap: Revival is not automatic in many jurisdictions.

rules.title: Lost Will Presumption
rules.prompt_question: What presumption applies when a will last in the testator's possession cannot be found at death?
rules.rule_statement: If a will last known to be in the testator's possession cannot be found at death, there is a rebuttable presumption that the testator destroyed it with intent to revoke.
rules.application_example: Family members cannot find the original will in the decedent's safe after death.
rules.common_trap: The presumption may be rebutted by evidence of accidental loss or destruction by another.

rules.title: Ademption by Extinction
rules.prompt_question: What is ademption by extinction?
rules.rule_statement: If specifically devised property is not in the estate at the testator's death, the specific gift generally fails by ademption, subject to statutory exceptions and replacement-property rules.
rules.application_example: A will leaves 'my diamond ring to Ana,' but the ring was sold before death.
rules.common_trap: Ademption generally applies only to specific devises, not general or demonstrative gifts.

rules.title: Ademption Exceptions
rules.prompt_question: What are common exceptions to ademption by extinction?
rules.rule_statement: Many statutes provide that a specific devisee may receive replacement property, insurance proceeds, condemnation awards, or sale proceeds unpaid at death, and if a conservator sold specifically devised property while the testator lacked capacity, the devisee may be entitled to traceable sale proceeds.
rules.application_example: A conservator sells the testator's specifically devised house during incapacity, and the devisee may receive the remaining sale proceeds.
rules.common_trap: Modern statutes soften harsh ademption results, especially in involuntary sale situations.

rules.title: Ademption by Satisfaction
rules.prompt_question: What is ademption by satisfaction?
rules.rule_statement: A lifetime gift to a devisee is treated as satisfaction of a testamentary gift only if the will provides for deduction, the testator declares in a contemporaneous writing that it is in satisfaction, or the devisee acknowledges this in writing.
rules.application_example: A testator gives a child $50,000 during life and signs a note stating it is an advance on the child's testamentary gift.
rules.common_trap: Unlike ordinary advancements in intestacy, satisfaction of a will gift usually requires written evidence.

rules.title: Exoneration of Liens
rules.prompt_question: Must the estate pay off a mortgage on specifically devised property?
rules.rule_statement: Under modern law, a specific devisee generally takes devised property subject to any mortgage or lien existing at death unless the will directs exoneration.
rules.application_example: A devisee receives a house with its outstanding mortgage unless the will says the debt should be paid from the residue.
rules.common_trap: The common law often favored exoneration, but the modern rule usually does not.

rules.title: Abatement
rules.prompt_question: What is abatement and what is the usual order?
rules.rule_statement: Abatement is the reduction of gifts when estate assets are insufficient to satisfy debts, expenses, and devises. Absent contrary intent, property generally abates in this order: intestate property, residuary devises, general devises, and specific devises last.
rules.application_example: If the estate lacks enough cash to pay debts, the residuary gift is reduced before a specific bequest of jewelry.
rules.common_trap: Classifying the gift correctly is essential because the order of abatement depends on the gift type.

rules.title: Lapse
rules.prompt_question: What is lapse?
rules.rule_statement: A devise lapses if the beneficiary fails to survive the testator, unless an anti-lapse statute or another doctrine saves the gift.
rules.application_example: A will leaves money to a friend who dies before the testator.
rules.common_trap: Always check for anti-lapse statutes before concluding the gift fails.

rules.title: Anti-Lapse Statute
rules.prompt_question: When does an anti-lapse statute apply?
rules.rule_statement: An anti-lapse statute generally saves a devise when the predeceasing beneficiary was within a protected degree of relationship to the testator and left surviving descendants, who then take the gift by substitution unless the will provides otherwise.
rules.application_example: A will leaves property to the testator's brother, who dies before the testator but leaves children.
rules.common_trap: A gift may still lapse if the will requires survival or otherwise shows a contrary intent.

rules.title: Class Gifts
rules.prompt_question: How are class gifts treated when a class member predeceases the testator?
rules.rule_statement: If a gift is to a class, surviving class members generally take the entire gift unless an anti-lapse statute applies to a deceased class member within the statute's protection.
rules.application_example: A devise to 'my children' is divided among the surviving children, subject to anti-lapse for a deceased child in a protected jurisdiction.
rules.common_trap: Determine first whether the gift is truly to a class or to named individuals.

rules.title: Void or Invalid Gifts
rules.prompt_question: What happens to a void or invalid testamentary gift?
rules.rule_statement: If a gift is void or invalid, the property usually falls into the residue, or passes by intestacy if the residue does not dispose of it.
rules.application_example: A devise to an ineligible beneficiary fails and becomes part of the residuary estate.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse void gifts with lapsed gifts, though the ultimate destination may be similar.

rules.title: Failed Residuary Gifts
rules.prompt_question: How is a failed residuary devise treated?
rules.rule_statement: Under the modern rule, if one share of a residuary gift fails, the other residuary beneficiaries typically take the failed share in proportion to their interests rather than the failed portion passing by intestacy.
rules.application_example: A residue left equally to A and B results in all residue going to A if B predeceases and no anti-lapse rule applies.
rules.common_trap: Older common-law 'no residue of a residue' rules are often replaced by statute.

rules.title: Pretermitted Child
rules.prompt_question: When may an omitted child take despite a will?
rules.rule_statement: A pretermitted child, usually a child born or adopted after execution of the will and unintentionally omitted, may receive an intestate share or the portion provided by statute unless the omission was intentional or the testator otherwise provided for the child outside the will.
rules.application_example: A child born after the will is signed may receive a statutory share if the will was never updated.
rules.common_trap: Existing children omitted when the will was executed are treated differently from later-born or later-adopted children.

rules.title: Omitted Spouse
rules.prompt_question: When may a surviving spouse omitted from a premarital will take despite the will?
rules.rule_statement: A spouse who married the testator after execution of the will and is omitted may receive a statutory intestate share or elective provision unless the omission was intentional, the spouse was provided for outside the will in lieu of testamentary transfer, or the will was made in contemplation of marriage.
rules.application_example: A testator marries after signing an old will that leaves everything to siblings and never updates it.
rules.common_trap: An omitted spouse doctrine is distinct from the surviving spouse's elective share.

rules.title: Elective Share
rules.prompt_question: What is a surviving spouse's elective share?
rules.rule_statement: A surviving spouse may renounce the will and claim the statutory elective share, which is a forced share of the decedent's estate or augmented estate designed to protect the spouse from disinheritance.
rules.application_example: A decedent leaves almost nothing to the spouse, who elects against the will for the statutory percentage.
rules.common_trap: The elective share is not the same as intestacy and may include certain nonprobate transfers in the augmented estate.

rules.title: Homestead, Exempt Property, and Family Allowance
rules.prompt_question: What statutory protections may a surviving spouse or children receive regardless of the will?
rules.rule_statement: Many statutes provide a surviving spouse and sometimes minor children with homestead allowance, exempt property, and a temporary family allowance payable off the top of the estate, often in addition to intestate or elective-share rights.
rules.application_example: A surviving spouse receives a family allowance for support during estate administration.
rules.common_trap: These protections are usually prioritized and are separate from ordinary testamentary gifts.

rules.title: Surviving Spouse's Share in Intestacy
rules.prompt_question: How is a surviving spouse's intestate share determined?
rules.rule_statement: A surviving spouse's intestate share depends on the statutory scheme and whether the decedent is survived by descendants or parents, with the spouse often taking all if all surviving descendants are also descendants of the spouse and no other descendants complicate the distribution.
rules.application_example: A spouse may inherit the entire estate when the decedent leaves only joint children and no parent claim under the applicable statute.
rules.common_trap: The spouse's share changes significantly when there are descendants from another relationship.

rules.title: Shares of Descendants
rules.prompt_question: How do descendants take in intestacy?
rules.rule_statement: Descendants take the balance of the intestate estate by representation under the applicable per stirpes, modern per stirpes, or per capita at each generation system.
rules.application_example: Children of a deceased child may take that child's share by representation.
rules.common_trap: The method of representation can materially change each person's share.

rules.title: Strict Per Stirpes
rules.prompt_question: What is strict per stirpes distribution?
rules.rule_statement: Under strict per stirpes, the estate is divided into shares at the first generational level below the decedent regardless of whether anyone there survives, and descendants of a deceased child take that child's share by representation.
rules.application_example: If one child is deceased but leaves descendants, that child's branch takes the child's full share.
rules.common_trap: Strict per stirpes differs from modern per stirpes when no one survives at the first generational level.

rules.title: Modern Per Stirpes
rules.prompt_question: What is modern per stirpes distribution?
rules.rule_statement: Under modern per stirpes, shares are first divided at the nearest generation with at least one living taker, and the descendants of deceased members of that generation take by representation.
rules.application_example: If all children are deceased but grandchildren survive, division begins at the grandchild generation.
rules.common_trap: Do not assume all states use the same system of representation.

rules.title: Per Capita at Each Generation
rules.prompt_question: What is per capita at each generation?
rules.rule_statement: Under per capita at each generation, the estate is divided equally among living takers at the first generation with survivors, and the shares of deceased persons at that generation are pooled and divided equally at the next generation.
rules.application_example: Two surviving grandchildren and four great-grandchildren descending from deceased grandchildren may share under a pooling method.
rules.common_trap: This modern system tends to equalize shares among persons of the same generation.

rules.title: Half-Blood Relatives
rules.prompt_question: How are half-blood relatives treated in intestacy?
rules.rule_statement: In most jurisdictions, half-blood relatives are treated the same as whole-blood relatives for intestate succession, unless a statute provides otherwise.
rules.application_example: A half-sibling may inherit the same share as a full sibling.
rules.common_trap: Some old statutes distinguish half-bloods, but the modern trend is equal treatment.

rules.title: Adopted Children
rules.prompt_question: How are adopted children treated in intestacy?
rules.rule_statement: An adopted child is generally treated as the child of the adoptive parent and not of the biological parents for intestate succession, except where statutes preserve limited inheritance through or from a biological family in certain situations such as stepparent adoption.
rules.application_example: A child adopted by strangers inherits from the adoptive parents as a natural child would.
rules.common_trap: Stepparent-adoption exceptions are common and frequently tested.

rules.title: Nonmarital Children
rules.prompt_question: How may a nonmarital child inherit?
rules.rule_statement: A nonmarital child generally inherits from the mother automatically and from the father if paternity is established under the applicable statute, such as by adjudication, acknowledgment, or posthumous proof.
rules.application_example: A child born outside marriage inherits from the father after valid acknowledgment of paternity.
rules.common_trap: The issue is usually proof of paternity and compliance with the statute.

rules.title: Slayer Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the slayer rule?
rules.rule_statement: A person who feloniously and intentionally kills the decedent is barred from taking from the decedent's estate, and the estate is distributed as though the killer predeceased the decedent.
rules.application_example: A spouse who murders the decedent cannot inherit through intestacy or under the will.
rules.common_trap: Accidental killing usually does not trigger the rule.

rules.title: Disclaimer
rules.prompt_question: What is the effect of a disclaimer?
rules.rule_statement: A valid disclaimer causes the disclaimant to be treated as having predeceased the decedent for purposes of the disclaimed interest.
rules.application_example: An heir refuses an inheritance for tax reasons and is treated as though the heir died before the decedent.
rules.common_trap: Disclaimers must comply with statutory timing and formal requirements.

rules.title: Advancements
rules.prompt_question: When is a lifetime gift treated as an advancement in intestacy?
rules.rule_statement: A lifetime gift to an heir is treated as an advancement against the heir's intestate share only if declared in a contemporaneous writing by the decedent or acknowledged in writing by the heir.
rules.application_example: A parent gives a child money during life and signs a note stating it should count against the child's eventual intestate share.
rules.common_trap: Advancement requires writing; mere generosity is not enough.

rules.title: Elements of a Valid Trust
rules.prompt_question: What elements are required to create a valid trust?
rules.rule_statement: A valid trust generally requires a settlor with capacity, present intent to create a trust, identifiable trust property, ascertainable beneficiaries unless the trust is charitable or honorary where allowed, a valid trust purpose, and compliance with any required formalities.
rules.application_example: A settlor transfers cash to a trustee to hold and invest for named children.
rules.common_trap: A mere moral obligation or future intention to create a trust is not enough.

rules.title: Intent to Create a Trust
rules.prompt_question: What intent is required to create a trust?
rules.rule_statement: The settlor must manifest present intent to impose enforceable fiduciary duties on a trustee to hold property for beneficiaries.
rules.application_example: A statement that 'I hold this property in trust for my son' may create a trust if supported by the circumstances.
rules.common_trap: Precatory words alone, such as 'wish' or 'hope,' may be insufficient without clear intent.

rules.title: Trust Property
rules.prompt_question: What property is needed to create a trust?
rules.rule_statement: A trust must have trust property, also called the res, that is presently identified and transferable to the trustee or held by the settlor as trustee.
rules.application_example: A settlor transfers a brokerage account into trust for a child.
rules.common_trap: A promise to create a trust with future-acquired property usually does not create a present trust.

rules.title: Ascertainable Beneficiaries
rules.prompt_question: What beneficiary requirement generally applies to private trusts?
rules.rule_statement: A private trust generally must have definite or reasonably ascertainable beneficiaries so the trustee's duties can be enforced.
rules.application_example: A trust for 'my children' has sufficiently definite beneficiaries.
rules.common_trap: A trust for 'my friends' may fail for indefiniteness unless the group is objectively identifiable.

rules.title: Charitable Trust
rules.prompt_question: What is a charitable trust?
rules.rule_statement: A charitable trust is a trust created for a charitable purpose benefiting the public or a sufficiently broad segment of the public, such as relief of poverty, advancement of education, religion, health, or other community benefits.
rules.application_example: A trust funding scholarships for local students is charitable.
rules.common_trap: Because a charitable trust serves the public, it need not have individually identifiable beneficiaries.

rules.title: Cy Pres
rules.prompt_question: When does cy pres apply to a charitable trust?
rules.rule_statement: If a charitable purpose becomes unlawful, impossible, impracticable, or wasteful, a court may apply cy pres to modify the trust in a manner as near as possible to the settlor's charitable intent.
rules.application_example: A trust for a hospital that no longer exists may be redirected to a similar medical charity.
rules.common_trap: Cy pres requires general charitable intent; otherwise the property may revert or pass by resulting trust.

rules.title: Honorary Trust
rules.prompt_question: What is an honorary trust?
rules.rule_statement: An honorary trust is a noncharitable trust without an enforceable human beneficiary, often for a pet or grave maintenance, and is valid only to the extent permitted by statute or modern doctrine.
rules.application_example: A trust to care for the settlor's dog after death may be valid under modern pet-trust statutes.
rules.common_trap: Traditional common law treated many honorary trusts as unenforceable unless saved by statute.

rules.title: Resulting Trust
rules.prompt_question: When does a resulting trust arise?
rules.rule_statement: A resulting trust arises when a trust fails in whole or part or when circumstances show the transferee was not intended to take beneficially, so the property returns to the settlor or the settlor's estate.
rules.application_example: A private trust fails for lack of beneficiaries and the trust property returns to the settlor's estate.
rules.common_trap: A resulting trust is a gap-filling equitable remedy, not a trust based on wrongdoing.

rules.title: Constructive Trust
rules.prompt_question: What is a constructive trust?
rules.rule_statement: A constructive trust is an equitable remedy imposed to prevent unjust enrichment when a person acquired property through wrongdoing such as fraud, breach of fiduciary duty, or mistake.
rules.application_example: A caregiver fraudulently induces a transfer of property and is declared constructive trustee for the rightful owner.
rules.common_trap: A constructive trust is remedial and arises by operation of law, not from the settlor's intent.

rules.title: Revocable Trust
rules.prompt_question: What is the default rule regarding revocability of trusts?
rules.rule_statement: Under modern law, a trust is presumed revocable unless the terms provide that it is irrevocable, although older law presumed the opposite in some jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: A settlor creates a living trust and later amends it because the trust instrument does not make it irrevocable.
rules.common_trap: The default rule may vary in older authorities, but the modern UTC approach presumes revocability.

rules.title: Amendment or Revocation of Revocable Trust
rules.prompt_question: How may a revocable trust be amended or revoked?
rules.rule_statement: A settlor may amend or revoke a revocable trust by the method provided in the trust instrument, or, absent an exclusive method, by any method showing clear and convincing evidence of the settlor's intent.
rules.application_example: A settlor signs a later writing expressly revoking the living trust in the manner the trust instrument requires.
rules.common_trap: If the instrument makes a method exclusive, other methods will not suffice.

rules.title: Revocable Trust as Will Substitute
rules.prompt_question: Why is a revocable trust often used as a will substitute?
rules.rule_statement: A revocable living trust allows the settlor to retain control during life while providing for management during incapacity and nonprobate transfer at death, often working with a pour-over will.
rules.application_example: A settlor places most assets in a revocable trust and uses a pour-over will for remaining probate property.
rules.common_trap: Assets not actually transferred to the trust during life may still require probate unless captured by the pour-over will.

rules.title: Trustee Duty of Loyalty
rules.prompt_question: What is the trustee's duty of loyalty?
rules.rule_statement: A trustee must administer the trust solely in the interests of the beneficiaries and avoid self-dealing, conflicts of interest, and transactions affected by personal interests unless properly authorized or consented to.
keywords: solely in the interests of beneficiaries
rules.application_example: A trustee may not sell trust property to the trustee personally without proper authorization.
rules.common_trap: A trustee's good faith does not excuse classic self-dealing absent approval or statutory exception.

rules.title: No Further Inquiry Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the no further inquiry rule?
rules.rule_statement: If a trustee engages in self-dealing, the transaction is generally voidable by the beneficiaries without any need to prove bad faith or unfairness, unless the transaction was authorized, approved, or otherwise excused.
rules.application_example: A trustee buys trust land at below-market value and the beneficiaries may set aside the sale without proving harm.
rules.common_trap: This is one of the strictest fiduciary rules in trust law.

rules.title: Trustee Duty of Prudence
rules.prompt_question: What is the trustee's duty of prudence?
rules.rule_statement: A trustee must administer the trust with the care, skill, and caution that a prudent person would use in similar circumstances, taking into account the purposes, terms, and beneficiaries of the trust.
rules.application_example: A trustee managing a trust for minors must invest and distribute with care appropriate to long-term needs.
rules.common_trap: A trustee with special skills may be held to a higher standard.

rules.title: Prudent Investor Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the prudent investor rule?
rules.rule_statement: Under the prudent investor rule, a trustee must invest and manage trust assets as a prudent investor would, by considering risk and return objectives reasonably suited to the trust, diversifying investments unless special circumstances justify otherwise, and evaluating the portfolio as a whole.
rules.application_example: A trustee spreads investments across different asset classes rather than putting all funds into one speculative stock.
rules.common_trap: Modern prudence focuses on overall portfolio strategy rather than judging each investment in isolation.

rules.title: Duty of Impartiality
rules.prompt_question: What is the trustee's duty of impartiality?
rules.rule_statement: If a trust has multiple beneficiaries, the trustee must act impartially in investing, managing, and distributing trust property, giving due regard to each beneficiary's respective interests.
rules.application_example: A trustee must balance the interests of a current income beneficiary and a remainder beneficiary.
rules.common_trap: Impartiality does not always mean equality; it means fair regard to the terms and purposes of the trust.

rules.title: Duty to Inform and Account
rules.prompt_question: What information duties does a trustee owe?
rules.rule_statement: A trustee must keep qualified beneficiaries reasonably informed about trust administration and material facts necessary to protect their interests, and must provide accountings as required by statute or trust terms.
rules.application_example: A trustee provides annual statements showing trust income, expenses, and principal changes.
rules.common_trap: Silence about major trust transactions may itself be a breach of trust.

rules.title: Duty to Segregate and Earmark
rules.prompt_question: What is the trustee's duty to segregate trust assets?
rules.rule_statement: A trustee must keep trust property separate from the trustee's own property and properly earmark it as trust property.
rules.application_example: A trustee opens a separate trust bank account rather than mixing trust funds with personal funds.
rules.common_trap: Commingling may expose the trustee to strict liability if losses occur.

rules.title: Delegation by Trustee
rules.prompt_question: When may a trustee delegate duties?
rules.rule_statement: A trustee may delegate investment and management functions that a prudent trustee of comparable skills could properly delegate, but must use reasonable care in selecting the agent, setting the scope of delegation, and reviewing the agent's actions.
rules.application_example: A trustee hires a professional investment advisor and monitors the advisor's performance.
rules.common_trap: A trustee cannot blindly hand over responsibilities without supervision.

rules.title: Remedies for Breach of Trust
rules.prompt_question: What remedies are available for breach of trust?
rules.rule_statement: For breach of trust, a court may compel performance, enjoin breaches, order redress by money damages or restoration of property, trace and recover trust assets, deny compensation, remove the trustee, or impose other equitable relief.
rules.application_example: A trustee who made an imprudent investment may be surcharged for the loss.
rules.common_trap: Beneficiaries often may choose between damages and profit disgorgement when the trustee profited from the breach.

rules.title: Exculpatory Clause
rules.prompt_question: When is an exculpatory clause enforceable for a trustee?
rules.rule_statement: An exculpatory clause may relieve a trustee of liability for ordinary negligence in some jurisdictions, but it generally cannot excuse bad faith, reckless indifference, or abuse of a fiduciary relationship, and may be invalid if improperly inserted by the trustee.
rules.application_example: A trust instrument excuses a family trustee for good-faith mistakes but not intentional misconduct.
rules.common_trap: A drafting trustee who inserted the clause may bear the burden of showing fairness and informed consent.

rules.title: Removal of Trustee
rules.prompt_question: When may a court remove a trustee?
rules.rule_statement: A court may remove a trustee for serious breach of trust, lack of cooperation impairing administration, unfitness, unwillingness, persistent failure to administer effectively, or substantial change of circumstances making removal in the beneficiaries' best interests.
rules.application_example: A trustee repeatedly fails to account and ignores investment duties, prompting judicial removal.
rules.common_trap: Mere friction with beneficiaries may be insufficient unless it impairs administration.

rules.title: Spendthrift Trust
rules.prompt_question: What is a spendthrift clause?
rules.rule_statement: A spendthrift clause restrains both voluntary and involuntary transfer of a beneficiary's interest, preventing the beneficiary from assigning it and generally preventing most creditors from reaching it before distribution.
rules.application_example: A beneficiary's general creditor cannot attach future trust income protected by a valid spendthrift clause.
rules.common_trap: Certain preferred creditors may still reach trust interests despite a spendthrift clause.

rules.title: Exceptions to Spendthrift Protection
rules.prompt_question: What creditors may pierce a spendthrift trust?
rules.rule_statement: Common exceptions allow claims by child or spousal support claimants, providers of necessities, some governmental claims, and sometimes judgment creditors who provided services to protect the beneficiary's interest, depending on statute.
rules.application_example: A child-support claimant may obtain an order attaching distributions otherwise protected by spendthrift language.
rules.common_trap: The exact list of exceptions varies by statute.

rules.title: Discretionary Trust and Creditors
rules.prompt_question: What rights do creditors have against a discretionary trust?
rules.rule_statement: Because the beneficiary cannot compel a distribution from a purely discretionary trust, most creditors likewise cannot compel a distribution, though some exception creditors may reach distributions once the trustee decides to make them or if discretion is abused.
rules.application_example: A beneficiary's ordinary creditor cannot force the trustee to distribute principal from a purely discretionary support trust.
rules.common_trap: Discretionary trusts are often more protective than spendthrift trusts, but abuse of discretion remains reviewable.

rules.title: Support Trust
rules.prompt_question: What is a support trust?
rules.rule_statement: A support trust directs the trustee to make distributions as necessary for the beneficiary's support, education, health, or maintenance, and the beneficiary may compel reasonable distributions within that standard.
rules.application_example: A beneficiary may seek court relief if the trustee refuses to pay necessary medical expenses despite trust terms authorizing support distributions.
rules.common_trap: Creditors who supplied necessities may sometimes reach support-trust distributions.

rules.title: Self-Settled Trust
rules.prompt_question: May a settlor shield assets from creditors by creating a trust for the settlor's own benefit?
rules.rule_statement: Under the traditional rule, if the settlor creates a trust for the settlor's own benefit, the settlor's creditors can generally reach the maximum amount that could be distributed to or for the settlor's benefit, regardless of spendthrift language, subject to domestic asset-protection trust statutes in a few jurisdictions.
rules.application_example: A settlor creates a discretionary trust for personal support and later faces a creditor claim.
rules.common_trap: A spendthrift clause does not generally protect a settlor-beneficiary from the settlor's own creditors under the traditional rule.

rules.title: Power of Appointment
rules.prompt_question: What is a power of appointment?
rules.rule_statement: A power of appointment is an authority enabling the donee to designate who will receive certain property interests, subject to the terms of the instrument creating the power.
rules.application_example: A trust gives a surviving spouse power to appoint trust principal among descendants.
rules.common_trap: The scope of the power depends on whether it is general or special and on the donor's language.

rules.title: General and Special Powers
rules.prompt_question: What is the difference between a general and a special power of appointment?
rules.rule_statement: A general power of appointment may be exercised in favor of the donee, the donee's estate, the donee's creditors, or the creditors of the donee's estate, while a special power may be exercised only in favor of a limited class excluding those persons.
rules.application_example: A spouse may have a general power to appoint property to anyone including the spouse's estate.
rules.common_trap: A presently exercisable general power can have creditor and tax consequences.

rules.title: Standing to Contest a Will
rules.prompt_question: Who has standing to contest a will?
rules.rule_statement: A person has standing to contest a will if the person has a pecuniary interest that would be affected by the probate of the challenged instrument, such as an intestate heir or beneficiary under an earlier will.
rules.application_example: A child omitted from the latest will but included in an earlier will may contest probate.
rules.common_trap: A mere moral interest or disappointment is not enough for standing.

rules.title: Probate of a Lost or Destroyed Will
rules.prompt_question: When may a lost or destroyed will be probated?
rules.rule_statement: A lost or destroyed will may be admitted to probate if the proponent proves due execution, the contents of the will, and that the will was not revoked, often by clear and convincing evidence.
rules.application_example: A copy of a missing will is offered along with witness testimony about its terms and execution.
rules.common_trap: If the will was last in the testator's possession, the presumption of revocation must be overcome.

rules.title: No-Contest Clause
rules.prompt_question: What is a no-contest clause?
rules.rule_statement: A no-contest clause provides that a beneficiary who contests the will forfeits benefits under the will, but many jurisdictions refuse to enforce the clause when the contestant had probable cause to challenge the will.
rules.application_example: A beneficiary risks losing a bequest by filing a weak contest despite a forfeiture clause.
rules.common_trap: Enforceability and probable-cause exceptions vary by jurisdiction.
